author: deme1744
content: So the idea there is to be able to make changes to the object encoding of the input data but fork independent, right? That does seem like a good plan considering how this is still moving a lot.

author: jsign
content: Yes, this was actually possibl before but more like in a linear fashion (e.g. 0x0001 and 0x0002 could be both amsterdam, but maybe that was confusing). The SCHEMA_ID change is mostly to make it more clear using each bit for each of those two dimensions, plus adding a starting number for Amsterdam more coherent with provious fork history in case we want to prove older mainnet blocks further down the road.

author: deme1744
content:
I'm trying to think of how that could/would work practically.

So would the fork id part of the schema id then always refer to any underlying type in the `StatelessInput` that is already defined in consensus and execution layer specs. For example, `ExecutionPayload`, `ExecutionRequests`. We wouldn't ever want to change those independently of fork.

But the top level `StatelessInput` object and any stateless specific part could still diverge fork independent based on the encoding byte?

author: jsign
content: Yes, I think that's the right interpretation.

author: jsign
content: I think usually the lowest-byte won't change that much unless we do some "out of fork" change that is meaningful (independent of execution_payload)

author: jsign
content: so prob will be always 0xXX01 I think.

author: deme1744
content: ok, yes, that change makes sense then

author: jsign
content:
BTW, something we were discussing with  <@466102668138512394>  is that this thing of putting hte fork in SCHEMA_ID to easy deserialization (i.e. knowing which execution_payload is in StatelessINput) makes `chain_config.active_fork.fork` redundant and requires an extra check.

What do you think of potentially removing this field? (and only leave the `activation`? cc <@510413979080720385> <@912415191067676693>

author: jsign
content: as a refresher in the current PR (that we removed the blob schedule)

author: 0xrubo
content: my take is there should always be a room for sudden and unexpected changes. while we have that option, i'm fine

author: jsign
content:
I think we have three options:
1. Accept the PR as is, but need to check the first byte of SCHEMA_ID is coherent with that `fork` field. Which is redunant and a bit annoying.
2. In the PR also remove the `fork` field there
3. Do not use the first byte of `SCHEMA_ID` to indicate the fork  and rely on independent SSZ field read to read `fork` and know how to deserialize the execution paylaod (which I think many people find annoying, thus why we went using this `SCHEMA_ID` to ease that with the first byte now)

author: jsign
content: Any preferences?

author: deme1744
content: 3. I dislike the most, I'd prefer the have that prefix as close as possible to the decoding

author: 0xrubo
content: i need to double check the code, but at a glance it looks like the right thing to do

author: jsign
content: Which option you prefer from the three?

author: deme1744
content: I also think the removal of that field is fine yes

author: deme1744
content: It is literally the same enum value, right?

author: jsign
content: Yes.

author: 0xrubo
content: #2 but still wanna check my code

author: jsign
content:
I prefer 2. simply to have one less extra check and avoid incoherency -- from another angle feels a bit SCHEMA_ID lives in a different domain (e.g. decide how to decode) rather than internal EL logic activation.
But I think removing the redundancy might be worth it... since if someone misses the check could be dangerous. (So i think option 2. might be safer)

author: jsign
content: Sure, no rush. I can wait for you to check 👍

author: jsign
content:
> could be dangerous
The dangerous case are two forks where the `execution_payload` shape is the same, and some guest program does not check that `SCHEMA_ID[0] == chain_config.active_fork.fork`.
In that case, maybe something weird could happen. (If the two forks have different `execution_payload` shape, then the SSZ deserialization would at least fail)

author: deme1744
content:
For my understanding, is this the difference for 1 and 2?
1. Would make you use the fork ID for selecting the correct decoding + check on fork id in chain config validation. But what you are saying is that we also have to verify both to be the same, which might be missed. And is also a bit out of place as they are different things. 
2. Would make you do check on fork ID at decoding + use right decoding based on it.

author: jsign
content: Correct.

author: jsign
content: Option 2. is basically removing a redundancy, and thus a potential source for errors or missed checks that can lead to confusing the right fork or something quite bad. (Apart from just having redundan information in the input)

author: deme1744
content: Yes, that extra check in 1 makes it more ugly/annoying/error prone. It's even hard to wrap your head around the potential faulty situations of same decoding / different decoding, same fork / different fork, etc.😅
